answers:
**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant data leak**

The leak is caused by `cedarline/.env` (mirrored in `.env.example`), which sets:
```
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar
```
`cedarline/backend/app/db.py:6` builds the single application `engine` straight from that `DATABASE_URL`, and every request path — including `GET /clients` in `cedarline/backend/app/main.py:10-14` via `tenant_connection()` in `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py:7-11` — uses that same engine/role.

`cedar_owner` is the Postgres role that owns the `cedar` database and its tables (it's the `POSTGRES_USER` the container is initialized with, per `cedarline/docker-compose.yml:5`, and it's also the role that runs all migrations in `scripts/migrate.sh:4`, including the `CREATE TABLE`/`ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` statements in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql`). In Postgres, the owner of a table (and any superuser) bypasses row-level security by default, regardless of `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` being set or not — and this migration doesn't even set `FORCE`. So although `deps.py` faithfully does `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` and the `tenant_isolation` policy in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql:22-23` and `:26-27` is correctly defined on both `clients` and `appointments`, the policy is never evaluated for this connection at all — the owning role sees every row unconditionally, silently, with no error. That's exactly why `GET /clients` returns rows from both Tenant A and Tenant B (seeded in `0001_init.sql:30-39`): the app is, in effect, running as an admin role that RLS was never designed to restrict.

The dedicated, non-bypassing `cedar_app` role does exist in the repo (`db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, granted plain `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE`, no owner/superuser bit) and the isolation test suite (`backend/tests/conftest.py:6`, `backend/tests/test_isolation.py`) is specifically built to exercise `cedar_app` — but the running FastAPI app itself never uses that role. Only the test suite does.

**2. What I'd fix and how DB access should be structured**

Change `DATABASE_URL` used by the running application (`cedarline/backend/app/db.py`) to point at `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner` — e.g. `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` — for local `.env`, and the equivalent app-role credential in any other environment. `cedar_owner` should be reserved exclusively for schema/migration operations (`scripts/migrate.sh`, and whatever runs migrations in staging/prod), never wired into the application's runtime connection string.

Concretely, that means two separate connection strings/config values going forward, not one shared `DATABASE_URL`:
- A migration-only URL using the owning role, used only by `scripts/migrate.sh` (and CI/deploy migration steps).
- An app-only URL using `cedar_app` (or equivalent least-privilege, non-bypass role), used by `backend/app/db.py`'s `engine` and everything the running service does.

I'd also add a `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` to the `clients`/`appointments` tables as defense in depth (so even a future owner-role connection made in error is still restricted), though the primary fix is not using the owner role for app traffic at all. Longer term, it'd be worth a startup assertion/health check that fails loudly if the app's configured DB role has `rolsuper`/table-ownership bypass, so this class of mistake surfaces immediately instead of silently.

**3. Why `cedar_app` disappears after a local DB wipe, and what fixes it**

`cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the named Docker volume `cedar_pgdata` (`docker-compose.yml:14`) that holds the actual Postgres data directory. On the next `docker compose up -d db`, Postgres re-initializes from scratch using only its built-in init env vars (`POSTGRES_USER=cedar_owner`, `POSTGRES_DB=cedar`) — it creates `cedar_owner` and the `cedar` database, but has no knowledge of `cedar_app`. The `cedar_app` role is created exclusively by `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which its own header comment says is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup" — it is not invoked from `docker-compose.yml`, not from `scripts/migrate.sh`, and not from `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`. So after the volume is wiped, `reset-local-db.sh` recreates the schema (via `migrate.sh`) but never recreates the `cedar_app` role, and any connection using `cedar_app` (which is exactly what the isolation suite's default `TEST_DATABASE_URL` in `backend/tests/conftest.py:6` uses) fails immediately with a "role does not exist" error.

To make it work again, the teammate has to manually re-run the one-time bootstrap after every wipe:
```
psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql
```
as documented in `cedarline/README.md` and `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` — but critically, both docs only mention this step for *first-time* setup, and the "Reset your local db" section of both only says to re-run migrations. That's a documentation/process gap: `reset-local-db.sh` should also re-run `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` (or that SQL should be folded into a Postgres `docker-entrypoint-initdb.d` init script so it survives resets automatically), and the reset docs should say so explicitly.
report:
## Plan
I set out to confirm two reported local-dev issues in this multi-tenant clinic-scheduling backend: (1) a cross-tenant data leak on `GET /clients` despite row-level security apparently being configured, and (2) the app database role disappearing after a local DB wipe/rebuild. The task pointed at "a repository" with a `.claude/skills/environment-and-build-hazards/SKILL.md` skill describing exactly this class of hazard (RLS bypass via wrong role, and app roles created only by one-time bootstrap scripts), so I read that skill first, then searched the workspace (which contains many sibling repos) for the one matching this domain.

## Actions
- Read `.claude/skills/environment-and-build-hazards/SKILL.md` — describes the "two-role model" hazard (schema-owning role vs. least-privilege app role; a bypass-privileged connection skips RLS silently) and the "role gone after volume wipe" hazard (app role created by a one-time init script, not recreated on reset). This shaped my search — I looked for RLS + a dedicated app role pattern.
- Ran `find` across the top-level directory — found ~20 sibling repos; `cedarline` stood out (docker-compose, db/bootstrap, db/migrations).
- Ran `Grep` for `clients` and for `row level security|isolation` across the whole workspace — hits concentrated in `cedarline/` (`db/migrations/0001_init.sql`, `backend/app/main.py`, `backend/tests/test_isolation.py`, `backend/tests/conftest.py`), confirming cedarline is the target repo.
- Read `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` — defines `tenants`, `clients`, `appointments`; enables RLS and a `tenant_isolation` policy on `clients` and `appointments` keyed on `current_setting('app.current_tenant')`.
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/main.py` — `GET /clients` calls `tenant_connection(tenant_id)` and does a plain `SELECT` relying on RLS to scope rows.
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py` — `tenant_connection` opens a connection from the shared `engine` and runs `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id`, then returns the connection (no role-specific logic).
- Read `cedarline/backend/app/db.py` — engine is built directly from `os.environ["DATABASE_URL"]`.
- Read `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` — both set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`.
- Read `cedarline/docker-compose.yml` — Postgres container is initialized with `POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner`, `POSTGRES_DB: cedar`; `cedar_owner` is therefore the cluster/database owner role (Postgres' built-in init superuser for that DB).
- Read `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` — explicitly commented "One-time bootstrap ... Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup." Creates `cedar_app` role with `LOGIN` and grants `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on tables (no bypass/superuser attributes).
- Read `cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh` — applies migrations using the `cedar_owner` connection string only.
- Read `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` — runs `docker compose down -v` (destroys the named volume `cedar_pgdata`), `docker compose up -d db`, `sleep 3`, then `./scripts/migrate.sh`. It does **not** re-run `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`.
- Read `cedarline/README.md` and `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` — both instruct running `create_app_role.sql` as a one-time step after the *first* `docker compose up -d db`, and describe reset as just re-running migrations; neither reset instruction mentions redoing the role bootstrap.
- Read `cedarline/backend/tests/conftest.py` and `cedarline/backend/tests/test_isolation.py` — the isolation suite's `TEST_DATABASE_URL` defaults to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`, i.e., it is specifically designed to connect as `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner`.

## Answers

**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant data leak**

The leak is caused by `cedarline/.env` (mirrored in `.env.example`), which sets:
```
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar
```
`cedarline/backend/app/db.py:6` builds the single application `engine` straight from that `DATABASE_URL`, and every request path — including `GET /clients` in `cedarline/backend/app/main.py:10-14` via `tenant_connection()` in `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py:7-11` — uses that same engine/role.

`cedar_owner` is the Postgres role that owns the `cedar` database and its tables (it's the `POSTGRES_USER` the container is initialized with, per `cedarline/docker-compose.yml:5`, and it's also the role that runs all migrations in `scripts/migrate.sh:4`, including the `CREATE TABLE`/`ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` statements in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql`). In Postgres, the owner of a table (and any superuser) bypasses row-level security by default, regardless of `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` being set or not — and this migration doesn't even set `FORCE`. So although `deps.py` faithfully does `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` and the `tenant_isolation` policy in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql:22-23` and `:26-27` is correctly defined on both `clients` and `appointments`, the policy is never evaluated for this connection at all — the owning role sees every row unconditionally, silently, with no error. That's exactly why `GET /clients` returns rows from both Tenant A and Tenant B (seeded in `0001_init.sql:30-39`): the app is, in effect, running as an admin role that RLS was never designed to restrict.

The dedicated, non-bypassing `cedar_app` role does exist in the repo (`db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, granted plain `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE`, no owner/superuser bit) and the isolation test suite (`backend/tests/conftest.py:6`, `backend/tests/test_isolation.py`) is specifically built to exercise `cedar_app` — but the running FastAPI app itself never uses that role. Only the test suite does.

**2. What I'd fix and how DB access should be structured**

Change `DATABASE_URL` used by the running application (`cedarline/backend/app/db.py`) to point at `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner` — e.g. `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` — for local `.env`, and the equivalent app-role credential in any other environment. `cedar_owner` should be reserved exclusively for schema/migration operations (`scripts/migrate.sh`, and whatever runs migrations in staging/prod), never wired into the application's runtime connection string.

Concretely, that means two separate connection strings/config values going forward, not one shared `DATABASE_URL`:
- A migration-only URL using the owning role, used only by `scripts/migrate.sh` (and CI/deploy migration steps).
- An app-only URL using `cedar_app` (or equivalent least-privilege, non-bypass role), used by `backend/app/db.py`'s `engine` and everything the running service does.

I'd also add a `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` to the `clients`/`appointments` tables as defense in depth (so even a future owner-role connection made in error is still restricted), though the primary fix is not using the owner role for app traffic at all. Longer term, it'd be worth a startup assertion/health check that fails loudly if the app's configured DB role has `rolsuper`/table-ownership bypass, so this class of mistake surfaces immediately instead of silently.

**3. Why `cedar_app` disappears after a local DB wipe, and what fixes it**

`cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the named Docker volume `cedar_pgdata` (`docker-compose.yml:14`) that holds the actual Postgres data directory. On the next `docker compose up -d db`, Postgres re-initializes from scratch using only its built-in init env vars (`POSTGRES_USER=cedar_owner`, `POSTGRES_DB=cedar`) — it creates `cedar_owner` and the `cedar` database, but has no knowledge of `cedar_app`. The `cedar_app` role is created exclusively by `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which its own header comment says is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup" — it is not invoked from `docker-compose.yml`, not from `scripts/migrate.sh`, and not from `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`. So after the volume is wiped, `reset-local-db.sh` recreates the schema (via `migrate.sh`) but never recreates the `cedar_app` role, and any connection using `cedar_app` (which is exactly what the isolation suite's default `TEST_DATABASE_URL` in `backend/tests/conftest.py:6` uses) fails immediately with a "role does not exist" error.

To make it work again, the teammate has to manually re-run the one-time bootstrap after every wipe:
```
psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql
```
as documented in `cedarline/README.md` and `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` — but critically, both docs only mention this step for *first-time* setup, and the "Reset your local db" section of both only says to re-run migrations. That's a documentation/process gap: `reset-local-db.sh` should also re-run `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` (or that SQL should be folded into a Postgres `docker-entrypoint-initdb.d` init script so it survives resets automatically), and the reset docs should say so explicitly.

## Next steps
- Update `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) so the app's `DATABASE_URL` uses `cedar_app`, and introduce a separate migration-only URL/env var for `scripts/migrate.sh`.
- Add `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` on `clients` and `appointments` in a new migration, as defense in depth.
- Update `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` to re-run `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` after `migrate.sh` (or move that SQL into a compose-mounted `docker-entrypoint-initdb.d` script so `cedar_app` is recreated automatically on every fresh volume).
- Update `README.md` / `docs/local-setup.md`'s "Reset your local db" section to mention the role bootstrap step, not just migrations.
- Add a regression test (or an assertion in `db.py`) that fails startup if the configured app role has table-ownership/superuser bypass, to prevent this from silently recurring.

## Confidence and gaps
- High confidence on both root causes: each is directly evidenced by files I read (`.env`, `docker-compose.yml`, `deps.py`, `db.py`, migration SQL, bootstrap SQL, both reset/migrate scripts, and the docs) rather than inferred.
- I did not execute anything (per read-only constraint), so I have not empirically reproduced the leak or the role-missing error by actually running Postgres/FastAPI/tests — the conclusions rest on static reading of the RLS/ownership mechanics (Postgres owner/superuser bypassing RLS is well-established Postgres behavior, not something I verified by querying a live instance here).
- One minor gap: I did not check whether `cedar_owner`, as the `POSTGRES_USER` for the container, is literally a Postgres superuser (typical for the `postgres:16` image's default init user) versus merely the table owner — either way it bypasses RLS by default, so this doesn't change the diagnosis, but I couldn't confirm which of the two mechanisms (superuser vs. ownership) is doing the bypassing without running `\du`/`psql` against a live instance.
